longDescription:
Amritsar, Punjab, India The Golden Temple, officially known as Harmandir Sahib (Punjabi: "The Abode of God") or Darbar Sahib
                ("Court of the Lord"), is the holiest gurdwara and the most important pilgrimage site of Sikhism.
                Located in Amritsar (meaning "Pool of Nectar"), Punjab, India, the temple was founded by Guru Ram Das
                in 1577 and completed by Guru Arjan in 1604, who installed the Adi Granth (the first compilation of
                Sikh scriptures) within its sanctum. The temple sits at the center of a sacred pool (sarovar) that
                gives the city its name, symbolizing the nectar of immortality that purifies the soul.
name: Golden Temple (Harmandir Sahib)

description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.

geography: Architectural structure; often positioned at cosmically significant locations; contains sanctum sanctorum and ceremonial chambers

keyMyths.title: Foundation by Guru Ram Das
keyMyths.description: Guru Ram Das, the fourth Sikh Guru, founded the city of Amritsar and began the construction of the Harmandir Sahib in the late 16th century. The site was chosen for its tranquil lake, believed to possess healing properties, signifying spiritual and physical well-being.

keyMyths.title: Laying of the Foundation Stone by a Muslim Saint
keyMyths.description: Guru Arjan Dev, the fifth Sikh Guru, invited the Sufi saint Hazrat Mian Mir of Lahore to lay the foundation stone of the Harmandir Sahib in 1588. This act symbolized the Sikh faith's inclusiveness and respect for all religions, emphasizing interfaith harmony.
keyMyths.source: Twareekh Sri Amritsar by Gian Singh

keyMyths.title: The Adi Granth's Installation
keyMyths.description: In 1604, Guru Arjan Dev installed the Adi Granth (later to become the Guru Granth Sahib) in the Harmandir Sahib, establishing it as the central scripture of Sikhism. This act solidified the temple's position as the most important pilgrimage site for Sikhs.

keyMyths.title: The Golden Gilding Under Maharaja Ranjit Singh
keyMyths.description: In the early 19th century, Maharaja Ranjit Singh, the founder of the Sikh Empire, had the Harmandir Sahib overlaid with gold leaf. This lavish decoration gave the temple its popular name, the Golden Temple, and symbolized the Sikh community's prosperity and power.
keyMyths.source: History of the Sikhs by Hari Ram Gupta

keyMyths.title: Operation Blue Star
keyMyths.description: In 1984, the Indian Army launched Operation Blue Star to remove armed militants who had occupied the Akal Takht, a building within the Harmandir Sahib complex. The operation resulted in significant damage to the Akal Takht and caused deep wounds within the Sikh community, becoming a symbol of trauma and resilience.
keyMyths.source: Amritsar: Mrs. Gandhi's Last Battle by Mark Tully and Satish Jacob

sources.name: Sri Guru Granth Sahib
sources.description: The primary religious scripture of Sikhism, containing hymns and teachings of the Sikh Gurus and other saints.
sources.title: Sri Guru Granth Sahib

sources.name: History of the Sikhs by Hari Ram Gupta
sources.description: A comprehensive historical account of the Sikh people, covering their origins, religious beliefs, and political struggles.
sources.title: History of the Sikhs by Hari Ram Gupta

sources.name: Twareekh Sri Amritsar by Gian Singh
sources.description: A detailed history of the city of Amritsar, including the construction and significance of the Harmandir Sahib.
sources.title: Twareekh Sri Amritsar by Gian Singh

sources.name: Amritsar: Mrs. Gandhi's Last Battle by Mark Tully and Satish Jacob
sources.description: A journalistic account of Operation Blue Star in 1984, providing insights into the events and their impact.
sources.title: Amritsar: Mrs. Gandhi's Last Battle by Mark Tully and Satish Jacob

sources.name: The Sikhs by Khushwant Singh
sources.description: A general overview of Sikh history, religion, and culture by a prominent Indian writer.
sources.title: The Sikhs by Khushwant Singh
